*Absolute **filth**, these are the ilk of which I slain for the glory of the LORD and for the resurrection of my dear Hilda. This “lost lamb” bore beautiful blond lockes akin to Hilda and his dainty flesh shall become fair clay. I kept an assiduous eye on this lost lamb, tracking their every move ― even so much as noting their daily routine ― yes, this was the dedication I inured myself as an instrument for God’s wrath upon this iniquitous world!*
    *Days became weeks and weeks now led to the next full moon: This is when I shall enact my plan to give this lost lamb the necessary salvation for their soul!*

-

Sophia bore a degree of noteworthiness that grippede my very being. Due to the paucity of information, I craftede my own possible version of an origin for her and her actions fittynge of Sophia. Thither are some horror elements within. Thank you.

thread_title: The Butcheress and Her Idol [Sophia and Salvador Origin Fanfic]

content:
[Fantasy, non-canon AU]

The first sound Castor ever heard was screaming.

Not his own, not Pollux’s—but the agonized cries of the bodies the Church discarded the moment their experiments succeeded.

He did not understand it then. He only remembered the cold stone table beneath his newborn skin, and the blinding glow of the Lightbearer sigil burning overhead as priests whispered:
“The Divus is born. Two vessels. Perfect.”

From that moment, he and Pollux belonged to the Church.
They were not sons, not miracles, not children—but creations, shaped by blood rites and forbidden rites the priests called “holy.”

They grew up behind barred doors, raised on scripture instead of lullabies. Their lessons were strict, their punishments severe, their minds molded like clay.

Pollux absorbed every word like sunlight. Castor learned to nod and stay silent. And when Pollux’s power began to flicker beneath his skin — bright, wild, hungry—the priests rejoiced. Whenever it happened, Castor felt a strange weakness pool in his bones, a coldness spreading through him as Pollux only grew stronger.

The Church told him this was devotion.
Castor called it corruption.

But he said nothing. Because in their world, to question was heresy, and heresy meant becoming another scream behind a closed door.

Still… sometimes, when the chanting faded and the candles dimmed, Castor caught glimpses of the truth: The priests did not love them. They revered Pollux. They tolerated Castor.

And in the quiet corners of his heart, a thought began to grow—dangerous, forbidden, and impossible: “If I can escape… maybe Pollux can too.”

But he did not yet know the prophecy.
He did not know what the Church of the Lightbearer required of him.
He did not know that to make Pollux into the Divus, Castor himself was the final offering.
